region: Phetchaburi Province, upper Gulf of Thailand
desc: Phetchaburi is one of central Thailand's most under-sold heritage towns: part old royal stop, part provincial market city, with cave shrines, a hilltop palace, serious dessert culture and just enough faded shophouse atmosphere to feel rewarding without turning into a full tourist circuit. It suits travellers who want historical texture and food depth between Bangkok and the coast, not a resort stay.

todo.name: Phra Nakhon Khiri
todo.desc: Ride or walk up the hill palace for breezes, monkeys and views across the flat province.

todo.name: Khao Luang Cave
todo.desc: Visit the cave temple when sunbeams drop through the roof openings for the most atmospheric light.

todo.name: Thai dessert stops
todo.desc: Try local sweets; Phetchaburi is known in Thailand for palm sugar and dessert craft.

todo.name: Kaeng Krachan National Park
todo.desc: Use a guide or car day for forest, birds and viewpoints when park conditions suit.

todo.name: Wat Mahathat and old-town lanes
todo.desc: Spend time on the flatter old-town side as well, so the visit is not only hill-palace and cave icons.

todo.name: Palm-sugar and khanom stop
todo.desc: Treat the city like a sweets destination, not just a temple stop, and sample the local dessert culture that gives Phetchaburi its identity.

wifi.note: Usually good in town hotels and cafes, but if you are day-tripping into caves, parks or rural dessert stops, mobile backup is still useful.

prac.fltNote: Phetchaburi is really an overland destination from Bangkok rather than an airport-led one, and that is part of its advantage: it works neatly by road or rail on a Bangkok-Hua Hin line of travel. Bangkok's airports are the realistic long-haul gateways, while Hua Hin is secondary and not something to build UK planning around. Holiday weekends and Friday-evening traffic can make the final transfer feel much longer than the map implies.
prac.lang: Thai; English is common in main tourism areas
prac.visa: British citizens can visit Thailand for up to 60 days for tourism, business engagements and urgent or ad-hoc work, and GOV.UK says your passport must be valid for at least 6 months with at least 1 blank page. From 1 May 2025, foreign travellers must also complete Thailand's digital arrival card online within 3 days before arrival.
alerts: Monkeys around hill sights can grab loose items.
Cave steps and heat are tiring in late dry season.
English support is lighter than in Thailand's headline tourist cities, which is part of the charm but also part of the friction.
National-park access can change with weather and conservation rules.

sweet: December to February is usually the easiest Phetchaburi window for palace and cave sightseeing, town wandering and onward overland travel without the worst heat or monsoon drag.
